mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

frontmatter.title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

The intrinsic path object on a type A, written \mathord { \vartriangle } A, is defined by setting x \cong _{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.

Given a dependent function f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, it suffices to show any other g \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx is contractible with centre f.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

Let A,B \colon \mathsf {PathObj} be two path objects. The binary product A \times B is equipped with the structure of a path object by setting \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A \times B} \mathopen {} \left ( u,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv x \cong _{A}u \times y \cong _{B}v and 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {refl} , \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {}.
The univalence property of A \times B follows immediately from that of A and B.

If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.

Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

mainmatter.p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x \colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.

frontmatter.title: Coherent remainder as a path object

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a function equipped with a section \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {}. We can equip the coherent remainder of \sigma with the structure of a path object as follows:

ol.li: We equip g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {}

For each \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }, we likewise equip \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f with the following dependent product of intrinsic path object structures: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}

li.p: We exhibit the latter as a cartesian family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0, \eta _1 \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } and \eta _{01} \colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^* \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _1 \sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{ f } \circ \eta _0 \sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

mainmatter.p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \eta : \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} =x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta x \right ) \mathclose {} = \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {}
mainmatter.p: Given elements \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _0, \chi _0 \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _1, \chi _1 \right ) \mathclose {} of \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma, the resulting type \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _0, \chi _0 \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta _1, \chi _1 \right ) \mathclose {} is definitionally equivalent to the following dependent sum:
Any embedding f \colon A \to B is propositional.

We must check that for every b:B, the fiber of f at b is a proposition. Fixing u_0,u_1: \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, b we must check that u_0=u_1. We let \mathopen {} \left ( a_0,p_0 \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv u_0 and \mathopen {} \left ( a_1,p_1 \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv u_1 so that we have p_0 \colon fa_0=b and p_1 \colon fa_1=b. Invoking the path object structure on fibers (), it suffices to exhibit the following data:
As f is an embedding, the map \mathsf {act}_{ f } \colon a_0 = a_1 \to fa_0 = fa_1 has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {act}_{ f } ^{-1} , \epsilon , \eta , \chi \right ) \mathclose {}.
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0 \cdot p_1 ^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0= \mathsf {act}_{ f } \phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{ f } \phi = p_0 \cdot p_1 ^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.

frontmatter.title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

mainmatter.p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {} be a section of a function f \colon A \to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
mainmatter.tex: \begin {aligned} P &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ P & \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } } \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
mainmatter.p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.

Let A,B \colon \mathsf {PathObj} be two path objects. The binary product A \times B is equipped with the structure of a path object by setting \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A \times B} \mathopen {} \left ( u,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv x \cong _{A}u \times y \cong _{B}v and 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {refl} , \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {}.
The univalence property of A \times B follows immediately from that of A and B.

Let A be a path object, and let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Prop} be a family of propositions. Then the dependent sum projection \pi _1 \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx \right ) \mathclose {} \to A creates a path object structure \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} on \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx as follows:

ol.li: we have \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x:A \, \middle \vert \, Bx \right \} \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to x \cong _A y;
the reflexivity datum 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } is given by 1_{ x } in A;
univalence follows from univalence of A and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

frontmatter.title: Display of cocartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cocartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be p_*b \cong _{By} b';

for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx}b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each Bx

frontmatter.title: Display of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be b \cong _{By} p^*b';
for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of cocartesian families (). Fixing a:A, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on B a with edges \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _B^{ 1_{ a } } b' \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b \right ) \mathclose {} and reflexivity elements for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b' is a proposition. As each Ba is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Ba}b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of Ba. Therefore, we conclude via .
Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called fibrant when it comes equipped with a cleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a restriction operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p^* \colon By \to Bx;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b witnessing the unit law that restriction along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called a cocartesian family when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

frontmatter.title: Contravariant sum of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the contravariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _{Bx} p^*v.

frontmatter.title: Covariant sum of an cocartesian family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cocartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the covariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \cong _{By} v.

We have seen in how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing path object structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} given a path object A. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible variations on the concept of “dependent path object”:

ol.li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
cocartesian families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
cartesian families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

mainmatter.p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased cartesian and cocartesian representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

Let A be a path object. A displayed reflexive graph over A is given by families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data 1_{ u } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } } b for every u:Bx. The displayed reflexive graph is called univalent when for each x:A the induced reflexive graph \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _{Bx} \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \cong ^{ 1_{ x } }_B \right ) \mathclose {} on Bx is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.

We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A for the (large) type of displayed path objects over A. We will at times silently coerce a displayed path object B \colon \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A to its underlying family of vertices B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}.
Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called a cocartesian family when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} of path objects is called fibrant when it comes equipped with a cleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a restriction operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p^* \colon By \to Bx;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b witnessing the unit law that restriction along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Our goal is to show that cartesian and cocartesian families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base. We will do this incrementally.

frontmatter.title: Display of cocartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cocartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be p_*b \cong _{By} b';

for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx}b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each Bx

frontmatter.title: The total path object of a displayed path object

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B: \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A be a displayed path object structure over A. The total path object of B over A, written A.B, is defined as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A.B} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _B^{ \alpha }v
mainmatter.p: Reflexivity data is given by assigning 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } , 1_{ u } \right ) \mathclose {}. Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} followed by univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Covariant sum of an cocartesian family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cocartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the covariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \cong _{By} v.

frontmatter.title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxy \cong G'xy that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} is a cocartesian family of path objects over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( Gxx \right ) \mathclose {}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Display of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object. Any cartesian family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be b \cong _{By} p^*b';
for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of cocartesian families (). Fixing a:A, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on B a with edges \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _B^{ 1_{ a } } b' \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b \right ) \mathclose {} and reflexivity elements for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b' is a proposition. As each Ba is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b':Ba \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Ba}b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of Ba. Therefore, we conclude via .

frontmatter.title: Contravariant sum of cartesian families of path objects

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be an cartesian family of path objects over a path object A. We define the contravariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total path object of the displayed path object associated to B as in . In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _{Bx} p^*v.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic path object on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is a cartesian family over \mathord { \vartriangle } A:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {act}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxy \cong G'xy that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( -xx \right ) \mathclose {} is a cocartesian family of path objects over the path object \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( G: \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( Gxx \right ) \mathclose {}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

A suitable path object on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( s:B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as sectionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {act}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

A suitable path object on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of path objects \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( r:A \to B \right ) \mathclose {} } } r \circ s \sim 1_{ B }, as retractionhood witnesses form a cartesian family over their underlying sections (). In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {act}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over the path object B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to \mathord { \vartriangle } A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a cartesian family of path objects.

Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a path object structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} given by intrinsic path objects in each component is a cartesian family over \mathord { \vartriangle } A:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} } using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x: \mathord { \vartriangle } A \right ) \mathclose {} } } { \mathord { \vartriangle } \mathopen {} \left ( fx=b \right ) \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p: x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} be a family of path objects. We equip the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} with the structure of a path object, setting f \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} g : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx \cong _{Bx}gx and 1_{ f } : \equiv \lambda x \mathpunct {.} 1_{ fx }.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum.

If f \colon A \to B has a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A, then a path object structure on A induces a path object structure on B, setting \mathopen {} \left ( x \cong _B y \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( gx \cong _A gy \right ) \mathclose {}.

We first prove the lemma for biinvertible functions, so that both the section and retraction are tracked by g. In this case, we may use singleton induction via the univalence principle for types to restrict attention to the case that f is the identity map, and the result is immediate.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

Let A be a path object. A displayed reflexive graph over A is given by families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data 1_{ u } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } } b for every u:Bx. The displayed reflexive graph is called univalent when for each x:A the induced reflexive graph \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _{Bx} \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \cong ^{ 1_{ x } }_B \right ) \mathclose {} on Bx is univalent. We define a displayed path object to be a univalent displayed reflexive graph.

We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A for the (large) type of displayed path objects over A. We will at times silently coerce a displayed path object B \colon \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A to its underlying family of vertices B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}.

The intrinsic path object on a type A, written \mathord { \vartriangle } A, is defined by setting x \cong _{ \mathord { \vartriangle } A } y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of path objects

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of path objects B \colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic path object on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

frontmatter.title: The total path object of a displayed path object

mainmatter.p: Let A be a path object, and let B: \mathsf {DispPathObj} \, A be a displayed path object structure over A. The total path object of B over A, written A.B, is defined as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A.B} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _B^{ \alpha }v
mainmatter.p: Reflexivity data is given by assigning 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } , 1_{ u } \right ) \mathclose {}. Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} followed by univalence of \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.
